story:
Matt Perucho decides today is the day he stops being the dorky Asian kid and becomes the guy who can win Lani Macatangay’s attention. In his pale-blue suit from last summer’s wedding, collar tight and shoulders pinched, he waits on the steps of Skyline High, three rubber balls in his pocket and a routine practiced all week. Lani glides past—anime eyes, electric blue-black hair, silver hoops flashing, musky honey scent—her laugh like wind chimes. Matt steps forward, squeaks out a greeting, and tosses the balls. His jacket chokes his reach; the pattern wobbles; the balls ricochet off Lani’s head. She fastballs them back. One, two, three strikes. Laughter. “Clown.”

“Juggling? Of all the dumb ideas,” says Sarah Ann, his best friend since middle school—big glasses, baggy sweatshirt, careful hands. She scoops a chip bag for the circling doves and tells him to try simply talking to Lani. But Matt wants a plan. In English they read dead, old white guys, not a manual for being noticed. He’s heard about Colleen Hoover on TikTok—girls crying over true love. Sarah warns those stories aren’t exactly romance and the relationships aren’t healthy. He begs her to coach him anyway. First tip: no juggling.

At lunch, Sarah’s got a creased copy of It Ends With Us and a notebook. Why Lani? Matt mumbles about Aunties who say a Filipina girlfriend is best—and confesses he’s hypnotized by Lani’s hair, laugh, legs. Sarah clenches her jaw but keeps going: “Matthew” sounds more dramatic than “Matt.” CoHo’s books lean on tragic heroes—money, secrets, rage. She calls out how messed up that is. They test tropes: friends with her brother? amnesia? sports team rivals? No. Forced proximity then: an elevator. It’s a bad idea—reckless and wrong—but they do it anyway.

They pour canned soup on the stairs to make “puke,” block the way with cones, funnel kids to the one janky elevator. “Two at a time,” Sarah says, maneuvering Lani inside with Matthew. The elevator clunks to a stop. Lani explodes—pounding the doors, nails wedged in the seam, breath tearing. “Let me out!” She’s claustrophobic; when she was little, her dad locked her in a box truck for hours. Matthew’s gut drops. He tries to calm her with slow breaths, calls Sarah to start it back up. When he admits it’s staged, Lani sees red and shoves him. He catches her just to stop the blows, holds her while she sobs, promising it’s almost over. The elevator lurches down. Doors open to the entire school watching. “Cheating on your girl, Clown boy!” someone yells. Lani pushes away, calls him a perv, and storms off.

The next day, Matthew says the Hoover plan was a train wreck. Sarah asks if he’s tried being himself. He calls himself boring; she disagrees. He finally speaks his truth: years ago, he fell off his bike at an intersection; a car swerved, crashed, caught fire; he ran. He never found out what happened and hates himself for it. Sarah peels off her hoodie for the first time. Burn scars ladder her arms. She was in that car. Her brother didn’t make it. It wasn’t Matthew’s fault, she says; her dad was drunk. If he hadn’t swerved then, he would have somewhere else.

Silence softens. Sarah lifts an apple, orange, and banana—toss, toss, toss—smooth arcs, easy catches. Kids point. “Learn from your girlfriend, Clown!” someone shouts. Matthew laughs. He’s been trying to impress the wrong girl. Aunties can be wrong. They agree to stop pretending to be tragic heroes. He takes her hands. “It ends with us.”
